doubted that Jesus had risen from the dead.<|26.82|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Peter and the beloved disciples had a foot race to the tomb, doubting and needing to see for And so all you parents out there, I just want to hear a thank you.<|8.44|> <|9.58|> Go ahead.<|10.54|> <|11.50|> Uh-huh.<|12.44|> <|12.86|> You're welcome.<|13.60|> <|14.22|> What that means is that the newly baptized.<|21.18|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> "<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The thanksgiving over water we will soon hear says,<|4.86|> <|5.04|> we thank you, Father, for the water of baptism.<|7.84|> <|8.58|> In it, we are buried with Christ in his death.<|11.26|> <|11.86|> By it, we share in his resurrection.<|14.34|> <|15.34|> Through it, we are reborn by the Holy Spirit.<|19.16|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Therefore, in joyful obedience to your Son, we bring into his fellowship those who come to him in faith.<|10.00|> <|11.56|> We come to baptism in faith.<|16.22|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.20|> We bring our babies and our older children in faith,<|6.20|> <|7.98|> choosing to enter these waters of death,<|12.34|> <|12.34|> resurrection, and rebirth.<|15.54|> <|15.54|> It is a choice.<|17.02|> <|18.74|> Sister Macrina Whittaker, a Benedictine,<|23.36|> <|23.36|> writes of baptism in her book, "A Tree Full of Angels."<|28.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The waters of baptism flowed over me, and no original sin was seen.<|8.76|> <|9.90|> Rather, the eye of God beheld a tiny mass of bones and flesh, soul and spirit, infinite possibility, pure process, new, empty, and free.<|28.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Free to choose good or evil, light or darkness, life or death, grace or sin.<|13.10|> <|14.04|> We are free to choose these things, and we, like Thomas, also choose belief or unbelief.<|25.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We are reminded of that choice every time we come together as a community and repeat this sacramental act.<|10.76|> <|11.30|> Our welcoming of the newly baptized and our reaffirming of our own choices as we join them in saying the baptismal covenant.<|21.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The choice to continue in the apostles' teaching and fellowship, in the breaking of bread, and in the prayers.<|9.60|> <|10.34|> The choice to seek and serve Christ in all persons loving our neighbors as ourselves.<|18.70|> <|19.84|> And of course, all of this only happens with God's help.<|26.66|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> With God's help and with Jesus' willingness to meet Thomas and to meet us right where we most need to be met.<|10.60|> <|12.22|> Thomas chose belief.<|14.62|> <|15.56|> My Lord and my God, he exclaimed.<|18.72|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Sister Macrina goes on to say in her poem,<|5.00|> <|5.00|> "It was my original union.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> I was passing through the baptismal waters,<|13.00|> <|13.00|> being filled with power like unto God's,<|18.00|> <|18.00|> and God wept at the possibility of me."<|23.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And God wept at the possibility of me.<|5.82|> <|7.26|> How amazing is that notion?<|10.38|> <|11.34|> I think most of us have probably wept at the possibility of God,<|15.88|> <|16.38|> but to imagine that God weeps at the possibility of us as well.<|22.38|> <|22.38|> Amen.<|22.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The possibility that we will again and again choose belief.<|6.62|> <|7.36|> That we will recommit again and again through the baptismal covenant to belief in God and Jesus and the Holy Spirit.<|17.66|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Re-commit with God's help to strive for justice and peace among all people, and respect the<|9.84|> <|9.84|> dignity of every human being.<|15.08|> <|15.08|> The poem continues.<|16.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Then somewhere in between my baptism and my daily life, my power like unto God's became scattered.<|10.50|> <|11.50|> I forgot my original union with God.<|16.08|> <|16.86|> And as I grew, I chose good and evil.<|22.12|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> light and darkness, life and death, grace and sin.<|9.66|> <|11.16|> Is it possible that Thomas is merely guilty of what we all suffer,<|18.80|> <|19.56|> the lapse in memory?<|21.44|> <|22.52|> I forgot my original union with God.<|27.54|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> These baptism Sundays remind us.<|3.92|> <|4.56|> They bring us together as a community and as individuals back into that union.<|11.98|> <|13.14|> The waters of baptism wash over us again.<|16.42|> <|17.36|> The water seeps under the closed doors of our hearts and minds, allowing Jesus to enter in again.<|26.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Although the doors were shut, Jesus came and stood among them and said,<|7.88|> <|8.54|> Peace be with you.<|10.20|> <|11.54|> Baptism puts us in that same space.<|15.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Sister Macrina finishes this poem called "Frail and Glorious" with these lines:<|8.00|> <|8.00|> "So on a day that felt like baptism, God gave me a glimpse of my hidden splendor,<|17.00|> <|17.00|> made me aware of that original union."<|21.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This day, this day of baptism, this day that feels like baptism, this is the day we welcome<|9.54|> <|9.54|> the newly baptized and remember our own baptism.<|14.02|> <|15.12|> This is the day we recommit to the covenant.<|18.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> the day that holy water seeps back into our life<|4.78|> <|4.78|> and reminds us of the possibility of us,<|9.70|> <|10.54|> the possibility of God,<|12.34|> <|12.94|> the belief we choose,<|14.74|> <|15.52|> and we are reminded that we are marked as Christ's own forever.<|21.66|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> more.<|1.14|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So dear Thomas, maligned year after year when all he did was ask for what all of us want, assurance and a reminder of who we are and whose we are.<|20.18|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A reminder that at baptism, God wept at the possibility of us.<|9.50|><|endoftext|>
